elements.sent-2411-602: The security of Bitcoin protocols is deeply dependent on the incentives provided to miners, which come from a combination of block rewards and transaction fees.
elements.sent-2411-603: This shift in Bitcoin’s incentivization mechanism, which introduces volatility into block rewards, leads to the emergence of new security threats or intensifies existing ones.
elements.sent-2411-604: This paper presents a reinforcement learning-based tool to develop mining strategies under a more realistic volatile model.
elements.sent-2411-605: We employ the Asynchronous Advantage Actor-Critic (A3C) algorithm, which efficiently handles dynamic environments, such as the Bitcoin mempool, to derive near-optimal mining strategies when interacting with an environment that models the complexity of the Bitcoin mempool.
elements.sent-2411-606: We revisit the Bitcoin security threshold presented in the WeRLman paper~\cite{bar2022werlman and demonstrate that the implicit predictability of valuable transaction arrivals in this model leads to an underestimation of the reported threshold.}
elements.sent-2411-607: Additionally, we show that, while adversarial strategies like selfish mining under the fixed reward model incur an initial loss period of at least two weeks, the transition toward a transaction-fee era incentivizes mining pools to abandon honest mining for immediate profits.
elements.sent-2411-608: This incentive is expected to become more significant as the protocol reward approaches zero in the future.
elements.sent-2411-609: As of December 2024, Bitcoin~\cite{nakamoto2008bitcoin holds a market capitalization of nearly 1.8 trillion dollars~\cite{market_cap , surpassing the annual Gross Domestic Product (GDP) of over 94\% of countries~\cite{gdp .}}}
elements.sent-2411-610: However, due to the halving mechanism, which reduces the protocol reward every four years, transaction fees are expected to match and eventually replace the protocol reward as the sole source of incentivization.
elements.sent-2411-611: Block rewards become volatile, as the inclusion of transactions with varying fee levels causes fluctuations in the total reward per block.
elements.sent-2411-612: For example, under the fixed reward model, once a miner receives a new block that extends the longest adopted fork in its view, the miner is incentivized to accept the new block and start mining on top of it~\cite{sapirshtein2017optimal .}
elements.sent-2411-613: Since block rewards are fixed, there is no advantage in taking the risk of orphaning an already-mined block.
elements.sent-2411-614: However, in a volatile block reward model, a miner might find it more profitable to attempt to orphan a newly mined block with significantly high transaction fees to steal those transactions~\cite{bar2022werlman, carlsten2016instability , a strategy referred to as undercutting.}
elements.sent-2411-615: Additionally, a miner may engage in selfish mining by creating a private fork of blocks to take advantage of including all available transactions from the mempool in its private fork.
elements.sent-2411-616: Some research has analyzed Bitcoin's security under the volatile block reward model in a simplified scenario.
elements.sent-2411-617: The study showed that the minimum hash rate required for a profitable deviation from honest mining, referred to as the security threshold, decreases as Bitcoin undergoes more halvings.
elements.sent-2411-618: In WeRLman, a block can include either a normal transaction or a whale transaction, resulting in blocks with only two distinct fee levels.
elements.sent-2411-619: This assumption simplifies the complex mempool environment to consider solely the count of whale transactions, all sharing the same fee.
elements.sent-2411-620: Moreover, WeRLman is designed to derive a near-optimal mining strategy only in a setting where the mining difficulty has been adjusted according to the adopted strategy.
elements.sent-2411-621: We begin our analysis by revisiting the Bitcoin security threshold presented in the WeRLman paper~\cite{bar2022werlman .}
elements.sent-2411-622: We demonstrate that, due to the design of the WeRLman environment, an adversary in this setting can predict the arrival of the next whale transaction before deciding on its action—an ability we refer to as predictive capability.
elements.sent-2411-623: We argue that the predictive capability strengthens the adversary compared to a real-world adversary, resulting in an underestimation of the security threshold.
elements.sent-2411-624: We modify the WeRLman environment to transform it into a non-predictable setting, where the adversary decides its action solely based on the mempool status at the moment of block generation, without any foresight regarding the types of subsequent transactions.
elements.sent-2411-625: The Bitcoin security threshold obtained under the non-predictable WeRLman model, while still lower than that in the fixed block reward model, is higher than what can be achieved under the original WeRLman environment.
elements.sent-2411-626: We proceed by introducing a simplified model to analyze mining strategies, in which the volatility of block rewards arises from the varying generation times of blocks.
elements.sent-2411-627: We argue that block reward volatility is not solely attributed to the infrequent arrival of whale transactions.
elements.sent-2411-628: The inherent competition among transactions to be included in the chain quickly causes blocks with longer generation times to include higher transaction fees, resulting in block reward volatility even in the absence of whale transactions.
elements.sent-2411-629: To this end, we build our simplified model upon the observed positive correlation between block generation time and its fee.
elements.sent-2411-630: Compared to the WeRLman environment, our simplified environment includes a greater number of blocks with varying fee levels, allowing us to analyze more fine-grained strategies.
elements.sent-2411-631: We employ a Markov Decision Process (MDP)-based tool to analyze the profitability of mining strategies under our simplified volatile model.
elements.sent-2411-632: Due to the limited number of states in the simplified model, the MDP tool can accurately measure an upper bound for the security threshold.
elements.sent-2411-633: The results of our implementation show that the Bitcoin security threshold under our simplified model is lower than the security threshold obtained under the non-predictable WeRLman model.
elements.sent-2411-634: In the next step, we analyze the profitability of mining strategies before difficulty adjustment.
elements.sent-2411-635: An interesting observation under the volatile reward model is that a deviant strategy, such as a selfish mining attack, can become profitable immediately after launch without needing to wait for difficulty adjustment.
elements.sent-2411-636: As discussed in the literature~\cite{grunspan2018profitability, negy2020selfish, grunspan2023profit, sarenche2024deep , under the fixed reward model assumption, a selfish miner incurs losses during the initial epoch of attack when the mining difficulty has not yet adjusted, the duration of which is at least two weeks.}
elements.sent-2411-637: This initial loss period is one of the main reasons such attacks have not been observed in the Bitcoin network.
elements.sent-2411-638: However, under the volatile reward model, this loss period is shortened or may disappear entirely, making selfish mining a more significant threat in the transaction-fee era.
elements.sent-2411-639: Using our MDP-based tool within the simplified model, we analyze the Bitcoin security threshold prior to difficulty adjustment, which has not been explored in the WeRLman paper.

elements.sent-2411-673: The WeRLman environment: In the WeRLman environment, two types of transactions exist in the mempool: normal transactions with a normalized fee of $1$ and whale transactions with a normalized fee of $1+F$.
elements.sent-2411-674: The model assumes that, at each event of mining a block $B$ with a new height, a single transaction is added to the mempool.
elements.sent-2411-675: This transaction implicitly represents the transactions that arrive in the mempool between the mining time of block $B$ and the next block.
elements.sent-2411-676: More specifically, once a block is mined, the WeRLman environment samples a new transaction and adds it to the mempool.
elements.sent-2411-677: This new transaction is either normal, with probability $1-p$, or a whale, with probability $p$.
elements.sent-2411-678: This implementation assumption in the WeRLman model ensures a constant transaction arrival rate but introduces a significant bottleneck that benefits the adversary.
elements.sent-2411-679: In the WeRLman model, at each decision-making point, the adversary knows whether the next transaction added to the mempool will be a whale or a normal transaction before deciding its next action.
elements.sent-2411-680: We refer to this property of knowing the type of the next transaction in advance as predictive capability.
elements.sent-2411-681: Our analysis shows that the implicit predictive capability in the WeRLman model allows the adversary to make more profitable decisions regarding whether to publish or withhold its blocks, thereby increasing its profitability compared to a real-word miner.
elements.sent-2411-682: Note that, in reality, miners do not have advance knowledge about the nature of future transactions.
elements.sent-2411-683: While mining a new block, miners frequently update the contents of their blocks to include the highest-fee transactions available.
elements.sent-2411-684: Therefore, once a block is mined, it includes the latest available whale transactions, and the miner is unaware of the arrival time of the next whale transaction.
elements.sent-2411-685: To determine Bitcoin's security threshold in the presence of real-world adversaries, we modified the transaction sampling method in the WeRLman environment to eliminate the effect of predictive capability.
elements.sent-2411-686: We refer to this modified version of the WeRLman environment as the non-predictable WeRLman, which is explained below.
elements.sent-2411-687: The non-predictable WeRLman environment: The only difference between the non-predictable WeRLman environment and the original version is that, in the non-predictable environment, the mempool includes only the transactions that arrived up to the block generation event, excluding future transactions.

elements.sent-2411-723: In this section, we introduce a new volatile reward model, referred to as the simplified environment.

elements.sent-2411-726: In the WeRLman model, the volatility of block rewards arises from the inclusion of whale transactions.
elements.sent-2411-727: However, it is important to emphasize that the primary source of volatility in block rewards during the transaction fee era is not the occasional arrival of whale transactions that offer significantly higher fees compared to others.
elements.sent-2411-728: Instead, the main factor driving this volatility is the competition among transactions to be included in a block as quickly as possible.
elements.sent-2411-729: This competition forces transactions to adjust their fees based on the state of the mempool.
elements.sent-2411-730: Over time, as more valuable transactions accumulate in the mempool, new transactions need to offer higher fees to have a chance of inclusion in the next block.
elements.sent-2411-731: Therefore, during periods of high demand, block rewards can rise significantly due to the presence of higher-fee transactions in the mempool.
elements.sent-2411-732: An important factor that increases transaction inclusion demand is extended periods during which no blocks are generated.
elements.sent-2411-733: Due to the random nature of Bitcoin’s mining process, block generation times can vary widely, ranging from a few seconds to over an hour.
elements.sent-2411-734: When block generation times are longer, miners have more opportunities to include higher-fee transactions in their blocks, thus increasing block rewards.
elements.sent-2411-735: This phenomenon is evident in the Bitcoin blockchain, where blocks with significantly higher transaction fee rewards compared to their surrounding blocks often correspond to those with unusually long block generation times.
elements.sent-2411-736: In our simplified model, instead of focusing on the concept of whale transactions, we concentrate on block generation time to analyze Bitcoin mining strategies.

elements.section-2411-016: Pre-DAM Selfish Mining Profitability Under Volatile Reward Model
elements.sent-2411-785: In this section, we demonstrate that selfish mining before difficulty adjustment can be profitable under the volatile reward model, enabling miners to deviate from the honest strategy for immediate gain.
elements.sent-2411-786: We first discuss the intuition of how selfish mining, whish is unprofitable under the fixed reward model, becomes immediately profitable and thus more threatening in the volatile reward model.
elements.subsection-2411-023: Selfish Mining Profit Lag Under the Fixed Block Reward Model
elements.sent-2411-787: Since Bitcoin's introduction, it has progressed without experiencing long-range or detectable selfish mining attacks.
elements.sent-2411-788: We argue that, in addition to the high hash power required for a successful attack, another contributing factor is the prolonged time required for selfish mining to become profitable under the fixed block reward model.
elements.sent-2411-789: As discussed in Section~\ref{sec:non_profit_before_DAM , selfish mining during the first difficulty epoch incurs financial losses for the adversary and must be sustained for several days to become profitable.}
elements.sent-2411-790: Figure~\ref{fig:minimum_day shows the minimum number of days required for a selfish mining attack to exceed the profitability of honest mining under the fixed block reward model, a period referred to as the profit lag in~\cite{grunspan2023profit .}}
elements.sent-2411-791: The figure considers adversaries with communication capabilities of $\gamma = 0.5$, $0.75$, and $1$ who follow the optimal selfish mining strategy from~\cite{sapirshtein2017optimal , which maximizes block ratio.}
elements.sent-2411-792: The main takeaway is that, under the fixed block reward model, selfish mining demands a significant time investment (more than one ideal epoch or two weeks) to become profitable.
elements.sent-2411-793: Under the volatile block reward model, selfish mining can yield immediate profits by shortening or eliminating the initial loss period, making it a more significant threat to the Bitcoin network.
elements.sent-2411-794: The primary reason for the immediate profitability of selfish mining under the volatile model is that the attack increases the average generation time of adversarial blocks before the first DAM.
elements.sent-2411-795: While this does not affect the protocol reward per block, it boosts the average transaction fee reward per adversarial block.
elements.subsection-2411-024: Pre-DAM Selfish Mining in the Simplified Environment
elements.sent-2411-796: In this section, we analyze the profitability of selfish mining before the difficulty adjustment under the simplified model introduced in Section~\ref{sec:simplified_volatile_reward_model using our MDP-based tool.}
elements.sent-2411-797: Let $\mathbf{t ^B_ideal$ denote Bitcoin's ideal average block generation time, and $R_\mathcal{A (t; \pi)$ represent the total reward included in adversarial blocks added to the canonical chain at time step $t$ under strategy $\pi$.}}
elements.sent-2411-798: Under pre-DAM selfish mining, the average block generation time (considering both orphan and canonical blocks) remains $\mathbf{t ^B_ideal$.}
elements.sent-2411-799: Since a new block is mined at each step in the environment, the expected elapsed time up to step $T$ is $T\mathbf{t ^B_ideal$.}
elements.sent-2411-800: By normalizing $\mathbf{t ^B_ideal$ to 1 and ignoring mining costs, the time-averaged profit of selfish mining before difficulty adjustment, i.e., the objective function, can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{eq: mining_time_averaged_profit} Profit^\mathcal{A}(\pi) = \lim_{T\to\infty} {\frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T}{R_\mathcal{A}(t; \pi)}}{T}} \enspace.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-801: Since, before DAM, the difficulty is not adjusted based on the adversary's strategy, the generation time of each block follows an exponential distribution with rate $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-802: Therefore, at each step of mining a new block $B$, we sample a random number from an exponential distribution with rate $\lambda$ to represent its generation time.
elements.sent-2411-803: Security threshold (upcoming years): To analyze the security threshold of Bitcoin in upcoming years, we need an estimation of the time-reward equation of Bitcoin blocks.
elements.sent-2411-804: The reward-fee equation takes the form $Reward(t) = R + fee_0 + r_{fee t,$ where $R$ is the protocol reward, $t$ is the block generation time in minutes, $fee_0$ is the base fee, and $r_{fee}$ is the fee increase rate.}

elements.sent-2411-949: Although the simplified volatile reward model introduced in Section~\ref{sec:simplified_volatile_reward_model improves upon existing models by accounting for multiple fee levels, it still has inherent limitations that hinder precise analysis of Bitcoin mempool patterns.. These limitations include:.}
elements.sent-2411-950: Linear assumption on the time-fee relationship.
elements.sent-2411-951: The fee accumulated over time by a block does not necessarily increase linearly.
elements.sent-2411-952: In practice, the time-fee relationship is often concave, suggesting that the rate of increase in block reward diminishes as generation time increases.
elements.sent-2411-953: Block reward estimation based on a single dependent variable, "block generation time".
elements.sent-2411-954: This assumption disregards the correlation between a block’s transaction fee reward and the generation times of preceding blocks.
elements.sent-2411-955: In practice, the prolonged generation time of a block not only increases its own reward but also affects the rewards of subsequent blocks.
elements.sent-2411-956: Imprecision in time-fee estimation due to sparsity of block data.
elements.sent-2411-957: Using sparse block data for time-fee regression (on average, one data point every 10 minutes) can lead to reduced accuracy in modeling mempool behavior for a specified period, causing deviations between real-time patterns and the model’s outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-958: To address the limitations of the simplified model, this section introduces our Asynchronous Advantage Actor-Critic (A3C)-based implementation, designed to derive a near-optimal mining policy while accounting for the complexities of a real-world Bitcoin mempool.
elements.sent-2411-959: While implementing the Bitcoin environment, we observed that accurately modeling the mempool requires a significantly large state space.
elements.sent-2411-960: This large state space is needed to store fine-grained information about the mempool state, particularly with respect to any blocks involved in a fork race.
elements.sent-2411-961: This expanded state space makes traditional MDP tools impractical.
elements.sent-2411-962: Consequently, we adopt A3C---a reinforcement learning approach well-suited for high-dimensional environments.
elements.sent-2411-963: The A3C algorithm~\cite{mnih2016asynchronous , introduced by DeepMind, is an actor–critic method~\cite{konda1999actor that leverages asynchronous parallelism.}}
elements.sent-2411-964: In A3C, multiple agents interact independently with separate instances of the environment and asynchronously update a shared global network.
elements.sent-2411-965: This parallelism not only accelerates exploration and learning but also enhances convergence stability in complex environments with high-dimensional state spaces, making A3C a superior option compared to traditional reinforcement learning methods.
elements.sent-2411-966: A3C enables us to implement an enhanced mempool, which allows for a more accurate analysis of mining strategies in the transaction-fee era by overcoming the oversimplifications of earlier models.
elements.sent-2411-967: Specifically, the enhanced mempool incorporates polynomial and logarithmic regression techniques to better estimate the time-fee relationship.
elements.sent-2411-968: The mempool state is dynamically updated based on the passage of time and block generation events, capturing the impact of historical activity on the current state.
elements.sent-2411-969: Additionally, it leverages fine-grained mempool data to model the time-fee pattern more precisely, enabling shorter and more stable regression periods that mitigate sample deviation issues.
elements.sent-2411-970: Additionally, a discussion on the implementation of the A3C-based tool is provided in Appendix~\ref{appendix:A3C_implementation .}
elements.sent-2411-971: This discussion includes a comprehensive description of the objective function (Appendix~\ref{appendix:objective_function ) and the A3C implementation details (Appendices~\ref{appendix: implementation and~\ref{appendix:undercut_implementation ).}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Updated function tryGetWithAndWithoutNewFee} \label{alg:tryGetWithAndWithoutNewFee} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Input: Current state $CS$ (self), and previous state $PS$. \State Output: Updated states $new\_with\_fee$, $new\_without\_fee$. \State $current\_max\_fork \gets \max(CS.h, CS.a)$ \State $prev\_max\_fork \gets \max(PS.h, PS.a)$ \State Create a copy of the current state: $new\_with\_fee \gets \text{copy}(CS)$ \State Create another copy of the current state: $new\_without\_fee \gets \text{copy}(CS)$ \If{$prev\_max\_fork < current\_max\_fork$ and $CS.pool < max\_pool$} \State $new\_with\_fee.pool \gets new\_with\_fee.pool + 1$ \State $new\_honest \gets \text{True}$ if $(CS.h - PS.h) > 0$ else $\text{False}$ \If{$new\_honest$} \State $with\_fee \gets 1$ if $CS.T\_h = PS.T\_h$ else $0$ \State $new\_with\_fee.T\_h \gets new\_with\_fee.T\_h + with\_fee$ \Else \If{$a \leq h$} \State $with\_fee \gets 1$ if $CS.T\_a = PS.T\_a$ else $0$ \State $new\_with\_fee.T\_a \gets new\_with\_fee.T\_a + with\_fee$ \Else \State $new\_with\_fee.L[-1] \gets 1$ \EndIf \EndIf \EndIf \State Return $new\_with\_fee$, $new\_without\_fee$ \end{algorithmic}

We have: \begin{equation}\begin{split} P_{0,0} = \frac{1-(1-\alpha)(\alpha+p)}{1-(1-p)\alpha(1-\alpha)}, \quad P_{0,1} = \frac{(1-\alpha)p(1-\alpha(1-\alpha))}{1-(1-p)\alpha(1-\alpha)}, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{\infty}{P_{i,i}} = P_{0,1} \frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha(1-\alpha)}, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{\infty}{P_{i,i+1}} = P_{0,1} \frac{\alpha(1-\alpha)}{1-\alpha(1-\alpha)}. \end{split}\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-1044: The normalized numbers of canonical honest blocks, canonical adversarial blocks, and the normalized adversarial block reward achieved under strategy $\pi_2^\text{Non-predictable}$ can be obtained as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{split} N_{\mathcal{H}} = P_{0,0} (1 - \alpha)(1 - p) + P_{0,1} (1 - \alpha) p + 2 P_{0,1} (1 - \alpha)(1 - p) \quad + \frac{P_{0,1} \alpha (1 - \alpha)^2}{(1 - \alpha (1 - \alpha))^2} + \frac{P_{0,1} (2 - p) \alpha (1 - \alpha)^2}{1 - \alpha (1 - \alpha)} \enspace, N_{\mathcal{A}} = \left(\frac{P_{0,1} \alpha^2}{1 - \alpha (1 - \alpha)}\right) \left( \frac{1}{1 - \alpha (1 - \alpha)} + 1 \right) + P_{0,0} \alpha \enspace , R_{\mathcal{A}} = \left(\frac{P_{0,1} \alpha^2}{1 - \alpha (1 - \alpha)}\right) \left(\frac{(1 + pF)}{1 - \alpha (1 - \alpha)} +(1 + F) \right) + P_{0,0} \alpha (1 + pF) \enspace . \end{split}\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-024: Selfish Mining Before Difficulty Adjustment: Supplementary
elements.sent-2411-1054: Figure~\ref{fig:percentage_increase_reward_3_18_december shows the percentage increase in the adversary's time-averaged profit before a difficulty adjustment resulting from conducting selfish mining.}
elements.sent-2411-1055: This figure is depicted based on different ratios of petty-compliant miners, over the mempool patterns observed on two different days: January 1, 2024, and March 1, 2024, both between 8:00 and 9:00 AM CET.
elements.sent-2411-1056: The figure is generated using the following configuration parameters: i) the protocol reward is set to $0$ BTC, and ii) the adversary's mining share and communication capability are set to $\frac{1 {3}$ and $50\%$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-1057: The greater percentage increase observed under the mempool pattern of March 1, 2024, can be attributed to a higher normalized rate of growth in fee rewards during this period.
elements.sent-2411-1058: Specifically, the ratio of the average fee reward for a block generated in 20 minutes to that for a block generated in 10 minutes is 1.29 under the mempool pattern of January 1, 2024, and 1.4 under that of March 1, 2024.
elements.sent-2411-1059: This indicates that if the adversary succeeds in orphaning a preceding block and thereby extends the block generation time for its own block, it can accumulate a relatively higher fee reward under the mempool pattern of March 1, 2024.
